title: Jealousy
summary: Explore the complex emotion of jealousy — where it comes from, when it becomes destructive, and how to manage it in relationships and daily life.

questions.text: Have you ever felt jealous of a friend or colleague? What triggered the feeling?
Is jealousy always a negative emotion, or can it sometimes be motivating?
How do different cultures view jealousy in romantic relationships?
What is the difference between jealousy and envy? Do you think the distinction matters?
Can social media make people more jealous? Why or why not?
Describe a time when jealousy led to a positive outcome in your life.
How should someone handle a jealous partner without hurting their feelings?

vocabulary.definition: The state of feeling threatened by a rival, especially in love or professional success.
examples: Her jealousy of her sister's success caused tension in the family.
Jealousy can destroy even the strongest relationships.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of discontent or resentment because someone else has something desirable.
examples: He watched with envy as his colleague received the promotion.

vocabulary.definition: Uncertainty or anxiety about oneself; lack of confidence.
examples: Her insecurity about her looks led to jealousy whenever her partner spoke to others.

vocabulary.definition: Demanding someone's total attention and love; unwilling to share.
examples: He became increasingly possessive as the relationship developed.

vocabulary.definition: Bitter indignation felt as a result of unfair treatment.
examples: Years of resentment built up between the two siblings.

phrases: Turn the green-eyed monster into a motivator.
Jealousy is a mirror that shows your own insecurities.
Compare yourself to who you were yesterday, not to others.
idioms: Keeping up with the Joneses — competing with neighbors or peers

quotes.text: Jealousy is the tribute mediocrity pays to genius.
quotes.author: Fulton J. Sheen

quotes.text: The jealous are troublesome to others, but a torment to themselves.
quotes.author: William Penn

teacher_notes: This topic works especially well for adult learners. Encourage students to share personal stories while being sensitive to cultural differences in discussing emotions. The 'jealousy vs envy' distinction is a great debate starter.

games.name: Emotion Debate
games.description: Divide students into two groups. One argues jealousy is always harmful; the other argues it can be beneficial. Students must use vocabulary from the lesson.

games.name: Scenario Cards
games.description: Write jealousy scenarios on cards. Students draw a card, describe the situation, and the class discusses how the person should react.

roleplay.scenario: A friend just got a promotion you wanted. How do you handle the conversation?
